strategic_overview: This is an intermediate 4-dimensional, 2-dilation rectangle problem: the standard width-volume estimates give necessary monomial inequalities, but not by themselves the mixed target factor S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4. The immediate baseline is Guth, Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles, https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403, Estimate 1 and Estimate 2, and The Width-Volume Inequality, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0609569, Propositions 3.3 and 3.4. For n=4, k=2 these imply useful lower bounds such as R_1 R_2 >= c S_1 S_2, R_1 R_2 R_3 >= c S_1 S_2 S_3, R_1^2 R_2 R_3 >= c S_1^2 S_2 S_3, and Vol(R) >= c Vol(S). But the desired extra factor sqrt(S_3/S_2) is a higher-order filling/linking phenomenon, not a plain width-volume consequence.

directions.name: Central 2-plane fibers plus relative filling volume
directions.description: This is the most direct proof strategy. Smooth f and take regular values y=(y_3,y_4) in the central S_3 x S_4 rectangle for pi=(x_3,x_4) composed with f. The fiber Z_y=pi^{-1}(y) is a relative 2-cycle in R. Degree one should imply that any relative filling of Z_y maps, using Dil_3(f)<=1, to a filling of the target 2-plane P_y=[0,S_1]x[0,S_2]x{y_3,y_4}; hence FillVol_R(Z_y) >= c S_1 S_2 S_3. The key missing lemma is then an anisotropic source filling lemma: under the negation of the two desired alternatives and R_1<=epsilon S_1, every relative 2-cycle in R with area < c S_1 sqrt(S_2 S_3) fills with volume < c S_1 S_2 S_3. Once this lemma is proved, coarea gives Vol(R) >= integral area(Z_y) dy >= c S_1 sqrt(S_2 S_3) S_3 S_4, contradicting the assumed failure of the second alternative.
key_ideas: Use degree to convert target filling-volume lower bounds into source filling-volume lower bounds for fibers.
Use rectangle isoperimetry to turn large filling volume of a 2-cycle into a lower bound for its area.
Use coarea for pi=(x_3,x_4) composed with f; since Jac_2(pi)<=Dil_2(f)<=1, the integral of fiber areas is at most Vol(R).
relevant_techniques: Guth thesis Area-Contracting Maps Between Rectangles, https://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/31158, Proposition 8.1.3: coordinate 2-planes in S have large filling volume.
Guth thesis, Proposition 8.1.2: small relative 2-cycles in a 4-rectangle admit cheap 3-fillings via a rectilinear random-point construction.
Guth, Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles, https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403, Theorem 3: rectangle relative isoperimetric profile, useful for proving the needed anisotropic filling lemma.
The Width-Volume Inequality, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0609569, Proposition 3.2: Dil_2<=1 implies Dil_3<=1, needed when pushing fillings forward by f.
directions.potential_obstacles: The pullback cycles Z_y and the statement that their fillings push to fillings of P_y must be made rigorous for a non-injective piecewise smooth degree-one map. The source filling lemma with exactly the threshold S_1 sqrt(S_2 S_3) is not quoted verbatim in Stage 0; it must be extracted from Guth's Proposition 8.1.2 plus the rectangle isoperimetric profile, probably with a case split according to R_2^2 versus S_2 S_3.

directions.name: Guth Chapter 8 solid-torus mechanism
directions.description: Adapt Guth's 3-dimensional solid-torus argument to the 4-dimensional relative setting. In the hard regime S_3>>S_2, ordinary volume-degree estimates are too weak. Guth's construction places in the target a solid torus modeled on a circle of length S_2 over a base S_3 x S_4; circles in the central base have filling area at least c S_2 S_3. For the present problem, cross this picture with the S_1 direction or use the relative planes [0,S_1]x circle(S_2). The intended output is: either the transverse parameter area forces R_3 R_4 >= c S_3 S_4, or the pulled-back cycles have enough area on average to force Vol(R) >= c S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.
key_ideas: The mixed exponent S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} is already present in Guth's boundary ellipsoid estimate B3a.
Filling-area obstructions are stronger than ordinary area comparison when S_3/S_2 is large.
The target family is parametrized by a 2-dimensional S_3 x S_4 base, matching the final coarea/integration factor.
relevant_techniques: Guth thesis Area-Contracting Maps Between Rectangles, https://dspace.mit.edu/handle/1721.1/31158, Proposition 8.1.1 B3a/B3b: for 3-dimensional ellipsoids, either R_3 R_4 >= c S_3 S_4 or R_2 R_3 R_4 >= c S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.
Guth thesis, Proof technique: solid-torus family of circles and filling-area obstruction.

Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants, https://arxiv.org/abs/0802.3550, Proposition 5.2: linked thick tubes as test maps with large linking invariant, conceptually the same obstruction.
directions.potential_obstacles: Proposition 8.1.1 is stated for diffeomorphisms of ellipsoids, while the problem allows arbitrary degree-one maps. Boundary estimates alone also miss the S_1 factor, so the argument must be coupled to an interior relative-filling or coarea argument.

directions.name: Almgren-Guth complexes of cycles for rigor and degree preservation
directions.description: Use complexes of relative cycles to replace informal inverse-image and filling arguments. Build a chain-level family from f, or from the fibers of pi=(x_3,x_4) composed with f, and prove that if all relevant 2-cycles have too-small area or too-cheap fillings then the complex is null-homotopic. This is the right framework for handling piecewise smooth maps, exceptional fibers, and arbitrary non-injective behavior.
key_ideas: Encode the family of cycles by a cellular chain map rather than pointwise smooth fibers.
Use relative isoperimetry to tighten higher-dimensional chains while preserving degree.
Conclude nonzero degree by showing the tightened complex cannot be null-homotopic.
relevant_techniques: Guth, Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles, https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403, Lemma 4.1: small-volume complexes of cycles in a rectangle are null-homotopic.
Same paper, Section 6 tightening construction and Key Lemma: replacing high-dimensional cells by isoperimetric fillings preserves degree.

Minimax Problems Related to Cup Powers and Steenrod Squares, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0702066, Section 1: gluing homomorphism for cycle complexes, giving a clean homological language for degree-one sweepouts.
directions.potential_obstacles: The published Estimate 1 from https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403 gives only the standard monomial inequalities and does not by itself yield the desired mixed factor. A solver must tune the tightening scale to the filling-volume obstruction, not merely rerun the existing proof unchanged.

directions.name: Cycle-space Sq_1 minimax route
directions.description: Formulate the desired inequality as a minimax-volume comparison in the space of relative 2-cycles. A degree-one map with Dil_2<=1 induces a 1-Lipschitz map on 2-cycle volumes, so natural cohomology operations on the cycle space give obstructions. The operation most likely to see the S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} transition is the lower Steenrod square Sq_1, possibly combined with cup powers to pack along the S_4 direction.
key_ideas: Ordinary width corresponds to the fundamental cycle-space class; the extra sqrt(S_3/S_2) likely comes from a higher cohomology operation.
Use target lower minimax bounds from many disjoint or antipodal rectangular regions.
Use source upper minimax bounds from bend-and-cancel or rectangle width-volume estimates under the assumed smallness of R_1, R_3 R_4, and Vol(R).
relevant_techniques: Minimax Problems Related to Cup Powers and Steenrod Squares, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0702066, Proposition 9.1 and Proposition 9.2: degree-one k-dilation compares minimax volumes of natural cycle-space cohomology classes.
Same paper, Vanishing Lemma for Steenrod squares and Main Lemmas 1-2: geometric pyramids force lower bounds for Sq_i classes.
Same paper, Appendix 3: conjectural anisotropic rectangle formula for V_R(Sq_{n-l}^Q a(k,R)); for n=4,k=2,l=3 this points directly to Sq_1.
The Width-Volume Inequality, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0609569, bending planes around a cubical skeleton: likely source-side upper-bound construction.
directions.potential_obstacles: This is conceptually robust but heavy. Stage 0 reports the rectangle minimax formula as conjectural in that paper, so one would need to prove the specific n=4,k=2 estimate rather than cite it. It may be overkill unless the direct filling-coarea proof stalls.

directions.name: Directional linking test-map comparison
directions.description: Construct a rectangle-adapted test map G from S, or from the double of S, to S^2∨S^2∨S^2 with a large second-order linking invariant. Compose with f. Since f has degree one, the invariant of G composed with f is the invariant of G. Then bound this invariant from the source side using Dil_2(f)<=1, directional coarea, and directional filling estimates. This is an explicit homotopy-invariant version of the minimax strategy.
key_ideas: A test map supported in linked rectangular tubes can be tuned to the side lengths of S.
Directional coarea and directional filling preserve anisotropic exponents better than ordinary isoperimetry.
The comparison is insensitive to non-injectivity of f because it uses homotopy invariance and degree.
relevant_techniques: Directional Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants, https://arxiv.org/abs/0802.3549, Proposition 2.2: directional filling and boundary-pushing for relative cycles in rectangles.
Same paper, Proposition 3.1: directional coarea inequality.
Same paper, Theorem 1 and Theorem 3: bounding second-order linking invariants from k_1-dilation and deriving degree-map dilation lower bounds.
Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants, https://arxiv.org/abs/0802.3550, Proposition 5.2: construction of large linking invariant from linked thick tubes.
directions.potential_obstacles: The existing theorem gives an ellipsoid-style monomial lower bound, not the exact rectangle dichotomy. One must build a relative or doubled-rectangle version of the test map whose invariant has the target scale S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4, and then prove the matching source upper bound.

key_obstacles: The central barrier is that standard 2-width and degree-volume estimates do not see the factor sqrt(S_3/S_2). That factor comes from filling or linking obstructions for families of 2-cycles, especially in the highly eccentric regime S_3>>S_2. A second barrier is functorial rigor for arbitrary degree-one maps: diffeomorphism arguments using inverse images and transverse area must be replaced by coarea, currents, or Almgren complexes of cycles. A third barrier is relative boundary control: the relevant cycles live in rectangles with boundary, so fillings must be relative and must keep track of boundary terms.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the central 2-plane fiber strategy. First record the baseline inequalities from Guth, Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles, https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403, Estimate 1 and Estimate 2, for n=4,k=2. Then prove the single local lemma needed for that strategy: assuming R_1<=epsilon S_1, R_3 R_4<=epsilon S_3 S_4, and Vol(R)<=epsilon S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4, any relative 2-cycle z in R with FillVol_R(z)>=c S_1 S_2 S_3 must have area(z)>=c S_1 sqrt(S_2 S_3). Try to prove this lemma by combining Guth thesis Proposition 8.1.2 with the rectangle isoperimetric profile in https://arxiv.org/abs/0710.0403, Theorem 3, splitting into cases by the scale of area(z) relative to R_1 R_2 and by R_2^2 relative to S_2 S_3. Once this lemma is available, the final proof is essentially coarea plus degree.
